items.evidence_text:
Client 2: Net, you mean? Like what hits the checking account?
Advisor: Good catch—gross before taxes. We can do net later, but for planning I want the gross annual number first.
Client 1: Gross household is about one hundred fourteen thousand a year—$114,100 if we’re being exact.
Client 2: I thought it was closer to like $120k, but—okay, yeah, $114k sounds right when you say it.
Advisor: Got it—$114,100 gross household. And how does that break down between you two—Emily versus Daniel?

Advisor: Okay—hang on, I’m just writing that down. Now expenses: what do you two spend per month all-in—housing, food, insurance, everything—ballpark?
Client 2: Like… five grand? Give or take.
Client 1: It’s not five. It’s closer to $4,500 a month total. I track it.
Client 2: Well—okay, $4,500 then. I was rounding up because it feels like five.
Advisor: Thanks, that’s helpful. So monthly expenses total: $4,500. When you say “feels like five,” is that because of irregular stuff—travel, repairs—or is it just the variability?

Client 2: Net, you mean? Like what hits the checking account?
Advisor: Good catch—gross before taxes. We can do net later, but for planning I want the gross annual number first.
Client 1: Gross household is about one hundred fourteen thousand a year—$114,100 if we’re being exact.
Client 2: I thought it was closer to like $120k, but—okay, yeah, $114k sounds right when you say it.
Advisor: Got it—$114,100 gross household. And how does that break down between you two—Emily versus Daniel?

Advisor: Great—monthly debt cost total is $0. Let’s shift to assets. Roughly how much do you have in investable assets—so not the house—across taxable, retirement, and anything else invested?
Client 2: Uh… seven hundred-ish? Maybe $700k?
Client 1: It’s higher. Total investable is about $767,800.
Advisor: Okay, $767,800 investable assets total. Let’s unpack that so it’s not just one blob. What’s in taxable—like a joint brokerage or bank investment account?
Client 1: Joint taxable is about $441,550. It’s at a bank.

Client 2: Yeah, there’s a private markets thing through an insurance company. About $89,350.
Advisor: Perfect. And then the house—just to acknowledge it even though it’s not investable—what’s the home value you use?
Client 1: About $277,500.
Advisor: Great. And you said earlier the life insurance premium feels big—Emily, is that a policy in your name?
Client 1: Yes. It’s my life policy.

Client 2: Net, you mean? Like what hits the checking account?
Advisor: Good catch—gross before taxes. We can do net later, but for planning I want the gross annual number first.
Client 1: Gross household is about one hundred fourteen thousand a year—$114,100 if we’re being exact.
Client 2: I thought it was closer to like $120k, but—okay, yeah, $114k sounds right when you say it.
Advisor: Got it—$114,100 gross household. And how does that break down between you two—Emily versus Daniel?

Client 2: Net, you mean? Like what hits the checking account?
Advisor: Good catch—gross before taxes. We can do net later, but for planning I want the gross annual number first.
Client 1: Gross household is about one hundred fourteen thousand a year—$114,100 if we’re being exact.
Client 2: I thought it was closer to like $120k, but—okay, yeah, $114k sounds right when you say it.
Advisor: Got it—$114,100 gross household. And how does that break down between you two—Emily versus Daniel?

Client 2: Net, you mean? Like what hits the checking account?
Advisor: Good catch—gross before taxes. We can do net later, but for planning I want the gross annual number first.
Client 1: Gross household is about one hundred fourteen thousand a year—$114,100 if we’re being exact.
Client 2: I thought it was closer to like $120k, but—okay, yeah, $114k sounds right when you say it.
Advisor: Got it—$114,100 gross household. And how does that break down between you two—Emily versus Daniel?

Advisor: Hi Emily, hi Daniel—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we get into numbers, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Hi Alex. We just want to make sure retirement still works with the fixed obligations—alimony is the big one—and that we’re not missing anything obvious.
Client 2: Yeah, and honestly I want it to feel simpler month-to-month. Less guessing.
Advisor: Totally hear you. Let’s start with the basics and then we’ll circle back to the alimony piece. First—just confirming—this is a two-adult household, just the two of you, right?
Client 1: Yes, two adults.

Client 1: Yes, “Other” works.
Advisor: Perfect, I’ll mark residence state as Other. Now, zooming out a bit—how would you describe your overall investing comfort level? Earlier you sounded like you can tolerate swings as long as the plan makes sense.
Client 2: I’m okay with growth, but I don’t want it locked up forever.
Client 1: We’re growth-oriented, yes. Pragmatic, but growth.
Advisor: Okay—so household risk tolerance: growth. Let me just sanity-check—when you say “growth,” you mean you’re willing to take market risk for higher expected returns, not just “grow the savings account,” right?

Advisor: Hi Emily, hi Daniel—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we get into numbers, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Hi Alex. We just want to make sure retirement still works with the fixed obligations—alimony is the big one—and that we’re not missing anything obvious.
Client 2: Yeah, and honestly I want it to feel simpler month-to-month. Less guessing.
Advisor: Totally hear you. Let’s start with the basics and then we’ll circle back to the alimony piece. First—just confirming—this is a two-adult household, just the two of you, right?

Client 2: We have the house, so… mortgage, right?
Client 1: No, Daniel—there’s no mortgage. The house is paid off.
Advisor: Okay, so no mortgage payment. Any other debt payments at all—minimums, installment loans, anything like that?
Client 2: No, none. We pay cards off.
Client 1: Zero monthly debt payments.

Advisor: Emily, Daniel—tax-wise, what bracket do you usually think of yourselves in? Not exact tax owed, just the bracket you plan around.
Client 2: Uh, I think we’re like… 25%? Somewhere in there.
Client 1: No—Daniel, we’re higher than that. We’re in the 35% bracket, roughly.
Advisor: Got it—35%. Thanks for the correction, Emily. Daniel, easy mix-up—bracket versus effective rate trips people up.
Client 2: Yeah, I was thinking what we actually pay, not the bracket. Sorry.

Client 1: We’re remarried—second marriage for both of us.
Client 2: Well—second for me, third for Emily.
Client 1: Daniel—no, it’s second for me too. Secondly wedded is the right label.
Advisor: Okay, secondly wedded—thank you. And thanks for catching that, Emily.
Advisor: Quick tangent—did you two have to fight traffic to get here, or was it okay today?

Client 1: It’s not five. It’s closer to $4,500 a month total. I track it.
Client 2: Well—okay, $4,500 then. I was rounding up because it feels like five.
Advisor: Thanks, that’s helpful. So monthly expenses total: $4,500. When you say “feels like five,” is that because of irregular stuff—travel, repairs—or is it just the variability?
Client 2: Variability. And the insurance premium—sorry, the life insurance—hits and it’s big.
Client 1: And alimony. That’s why I’m anxious about the cash flow.

Advisor: Okay—hang on, I’m just writing that down. Now expenses: what do you two spend per month all-in—housing, food, insurance, everything—ballpark?
Client 2: Like… five grand? Give or take.
Client 1: It’s not five. It’s closer to $4,500 a month total. I track it.
Client 2: Well—okay, $4,500 then. I was rounding up because it feels like five.
Advisor: Thanks, that’s helpful. So monthly expenses total: $4,500. When you say “feels like five,” is that because of irregular stuff—travel, repairs—or is it just the variability?

Advisor: Got it—$114,100 gross household. And how does that break down between you two—Emily versus Daniel?
Client 1: Mine is about $72,400 a year. I’m not working now, it’s more like ongoing income streams.
Client 2: And mine’s self-employed, around $41,700 gross.
Advisor: Perfect. And Daniel, when you say self-employed—business income coming in monthly?
Client 2: Yeah, it’s basically monthly business income.

Client 1: Yes—Emily is Client 1.
Client 2: And I’m Client 2, yep.
Advisor: Great. Daniel, role-wise in the household plan—would you say you’re the spouse/partner on the accounts and planning, with Emily as primary?
Client 2: Yeah, that’s fair—I’m the spouse/partner.
Client 1: Yep, that’s how we’ve been doing it.

Advisor: Okay. Now, a quick admin detail—Daniel, what’s your date of birth?
Client 2: June 24th… 1960.
Advisor: June 24, 1960—thank you.
Client 2: Wait—do you need the full thing like with the day? Or just the year?
Advisor: Full date is helpful—month, day, and year. You gave it, so we’re good.

Advisor: Hi Emily, hi Daniel—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we get into numbers, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Hi Alex. We just want to make sure retirement still works with the fixed obligations—alimony is the big one—and that we’re not missing anything obvious.
Client 2: Yeah, and honestly I want it to feel simpler month-to-month. Less guessing.
Advisor: Totally hear you. Let’s start with the basics and then we’ll circle back to the alimony piece. First—just confirming—this is a two-adult household, just the two of you, right?
Client 1: Yes, two adults.

Advisor: Are you currently self-employed, or are you an employee somewhere with a W-2?
Client 2: Self-employed. It’s my own thing.
Advisor: Okay. And what kind of work is it, broadly—like what occupation group would you put it in? Operations, sales, finance, something like that.
Client 2: Operations. It’s basically ops—keeping the trains running.
Client 1: Operations is right. He manages the day-to-day stuff.

Advisor: Got it—$114,100 gross household. And how does that break down between you two—Emily versus Daniel?
Client 1: Mine is about $72,400 a year. I’m not working now, it’s more like ongoing income streams.

Client 2: Yeah, it’s basically monthly business income.

Client 2: I thought it was closer to like $120k, but—okay, yeah, $114k sounds right when you say it.

Client 1: Mine is about $72,400 a year. I’m not working now, it’s more like ongoing income streams.
Client 2: And mine’s self-employed, around $41,700 gross.
Advisor: Perfect. And Daniel, when you say self-employed—business income coming in monthly?

Client 1: Sure.
Advisor: Okay—going back to what you said earlier about the business income driving most of the household cash flow, I want to make sure I’ve got each of your work statuses right.
Advisor: Emily, are you currently working at all, or are you basically not working now—retired, inactive, that kind of bucket?
Client 1: I’m not working now—so, yeah, inactive. Fully out of the workforce at this point.
Client 2: She’s retired-retired. Like, done.

Client 2: Uh—wait, isn’t that the same thing?
Client 1: Not always. But for us, both are zero. No balance, no payment.
Advisor: Exactly—thanks, Emily. So we’ll record it as no outstanding balances. One more check: any other property besides the primary home—like a rental or land—anything you’d count as property value?
Client 2: No.
Client 1: No, just the primary residence.

Advisor: Great—thank you. Daniel, you’re comfortable with that framing too?
Client 2: Sure, yeah. She’s the organized one.
Advisor: Okay. Now, Emily—this might sound like a weird question given you said you’re not employed, but what “bucket” would you put your occupation in historically? Like business owner, employee, government, that kind of thing.
Client 2: She’s not working though—so wouldn’t it be “retired”?
Client 1: No, he means what I did. Business owner is the right bucket.
